name: Glide Ratio Calculator
description: Compute glide ratio using lift and drag forces.
formulaDescription: Glide ratio is the distance traveled horizontally for each unit of descent and is approximated by L/D.

example.explanation: A lift of 4500 N with 130 N of drag yields a glide ratio of about 34.6.

faqs.question: What's the relationship between lift and weight?
faqs.answer: Level flight: Lift = Weight. Climbing: Lift > Weight. Descending: Lift < Weight.

faqs.question: How does angle of attack affect lift?
faqs.answer: Increases lift up to critical angle (~15-18°). Beyond that = stall. More angle doesn't mean more lift.

faqs.question: What's the difference between lift and thrust?
faqs.answer: Lift: perpendicular to motion, opposes gravity. Thrust: parallel to motion, opposes drag. Both needed for flight.

faqs.question: How do I calculate stall speed?
faqs.answer: Higher weight = higher stall speed. Shorter wing = higher stall speed. High altitude = lower air density = higher stall speed.

faqs.question: Why do aircraft have winglets?
faqs.answer: Winglets reduce wing-tip vortices improving efficiency (fuel savings 4-6%). Same span, better performance.

faqs.question: What's the best glide angle?
faqs.answer: Typically 15-20° from horizontal. Shallower = slower descent but longer distance. Steeper = faster descent.

concept: Flight mechanics calculators help analyze aerodynamic forces, aircraft stability, and performance metrics essential for aircraft design and operation.
guideSteps: Enter aircraft parameters (wing area, weight, altitude, speed).
Input aerodynamic coefficients (lift, drag, etc.) from design data.
Specify flight conditions (angle of attack, bank angle, etc.).
Click Calculate to see lift, drag, glide ratio, stall speed, and climb performance.
Use for aircraft performance prediction and mission planning.
standards: Lift coefficient (CL): Typical range 0.3-1.5 depending on angle of attack.
Aspect ratio: Higher values reduce induced drag (typical 5-12).
Stall speed: Minimum speed for sustaining level flight.
Glide ratio: Distance/altitude loss; higher = better efficiency (10:1 typical).
